entries.title: Inversion after negative adverbials
entries.explanation: In formal English, when a sentence starts with a negative or limiting adverbial (never, rarely, not only, under no circumstances, no sooner), the subject and auxiliary verb are inverted — like a question. It adds emphasis and a formal tone; very common in C1 writing tasks.
entries.structure:
Negative adverbial + auxiliary + subject + main verb
Not only + aux + subject…, but (also)…
No sooner + had + subject + past participle… than…
entries.examples:
Never have I seen such a beautiful sunset.
Not only did she pass the exam, but she also got the highest mark.
No sooner had we arrived than it started to rain.
Under no circumstances should you open this door.
entries.mistakes:
✗ Never I have seen…  →  ✓ Never have I seen…
✗ No sooner we had arrived…  →  ✓ No sooner had we arrived…
entries.note: Great for essays — use once or twice, not in every sentence.

entries.title: Cleft sentences (It was… / What I need is…)
entries.explanation: Cleft sentences split one message into two clauses to emphasize a particular part. 'It-clefts' highlight who/what/when; 'wh-clefts' (What…) highlight the whole action or thing. They are heavily tested at C1 in key-word transformations.
entries.structure:
It + be + emphasized part + that/who…
What + subject + verb + be + emphasized part
entries.examples:
It was John who broke the window, not me.
It was in 2019 that we first met.
What I really need is a long holiday.
What annoyed me most was his silence.
entries.mistakes:
✗ It was John which broke…  →  ✓ It was John who broke…
✗ What I need it is a holiday.  →  ✓ What I need is a holiday.
